title: Essential Tremor
oneLiner: A common, slowly progressive, bilateral action tremor (postural/kinetic) of hands ± head/voice; rare under 40, lifelong risk after 65.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy** – *single finding:* known diabetes + tremor + confusion/diaphoresis; bedside BM <3.0 mmol/L. Treat immediately with 50 ml 50% glucose IV (or 1 mg glucagon IM if no IV access).
- **Serotonin syndrome / Neuroleptic malignant syndrome** – *single finding:* tremor + fever + autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, myoclonus) + recent SSRI/antipsychotic use. Suspect; stop drug; get senior review – risk of rhabdomyolysis, seizure, death within hours.
- **Thyrotoxic crisis** – *single finding:* tremor + tachycardia (often >120/min) + thyroid goitre/exophthalmos + fever. Check TSH/FT4 urgently; treat with beta-blockade (propranolol) and carbimazole – arrhythmia/thyroid storm can be fatal.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral, symmetrical hand tremor – worse with action (holding a cup, writing, pointing) and sustained posture (arms outstretched), *absent at rest*.
  - Head tremor (yes-yes/no-no), voice tremor (quivering).
  - Tremor improves with alcohol (classic but not pathognomonic).
  - Gradual onset over years; family history in 50%.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No rest tremor, no bradykinesia, no rigidity, no postural instability (differentiates from Parkinson’s).
  - Tremor increases with anxiety, caffeine, fatigue.
  - Gait is normal (unless concomitant ageing).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Parkinson’s disease** – rest tremor (pill-rolling), unilateral onset, bradykinesia, rigidity, hypomimia.
  - **Drug-induced tremor** – recent/exposure to valproate, lithium, SSRIs, theophylline, beta-agonists, corticosteroids; tremor resolves on drug cessation.
  - **Physiological tremor (exaggerated)** – causes: anxiety, caffeine, hyperthyroidism, hypoglycaemia, alcohol withdrawal, fever.
  - **Cerebellar tremor** – intention tremor (gets worse towards target), past-pointing, dysdiadochokinesia, ataxic gait.
  - **Wilson’s disease** – early-onset tremor (<40), dystonia, Kayser-Fleischer rings, slurred speech; treatable if caught early.
- **Splitter:** **Rest tremor + bradykinesia** = Parkinson’s; **postural/kinetic tremor + head/voice involvement + normal gait** = Essential Tremor.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - BM (fingerstick glucose) – rule out hypoglycaemia.
  - Pulse, BP, temperature – look for thyrotoxicosis or serotonin syndrome.
  - Observe tremor at rest, posture, intention; ask patient to write a sentence.
  - Draw a spiral – assess severity (can repeat serially).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, glucose.
  - If young onset (<40): ceruloplasmin, serum copper, 24h urinary copper (rule out Wilson’s).
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **None** – diagnosis is clinical.
  - Second-line: **MRI brain** only if atypical features (unilateral, intention, early onset) to exclude structural lesion (e.g. cerebellar stroke, tumour, Wilson’s changes).
- **Special tests:**
  - **DAT SPECT** (DaTSCAN) – only if diagnostic uncertainty between ET and Parkinson’s (normal in ET, reduced in Parkinson’s).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Movement Disorder Society (MDS) criteria:**
    1. Bilateral action tremor of hands + forearms for ≥3 years.
    2. ± tremor of head, voice, legs.
    3. No rest tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, gait disturbance, or other neurological signs.
- **Never-miss test:** **BM glucose** – any tremor with confusion/sweating must have fingerstick glucose before anything else.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude and treat hypoglycaemia or toxidrome (see Killers First).
  - No specific therapy required for ET itself in acute setting.
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - If admitted for other reason: assess functional impact (eating, writing, dressing). Offer treatment only if disabling.
  - Start propranolol (first-line) if no contraindications (asthma, heart block, bradycardia). Titrate slowly.
  - Consider referral to neurology if atypical, refractory, or young.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **First-line:** Propranolol (see Drugs & Doses).
  - **Second-line:** Primidone (especially for hand tremor).
  - **Third-line:** Topiramate, gabapentin (off-label).
  - **Focal/refractory:** Botulinum toxin type A for head/voice tremor (must be done by movement disorder specialist).
  - **Severe/drug-resistant:** Deep brain stimulation (ventral intermediate nucleus of thalamus).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** No specific requirement.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Only if immobile – standard (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if weight >50 kg, avoid if high bleed risk).
  - **Diet/NBM:** No restriction unless undergoing procedure.
  - **Observations:** Standard NEWS chart – no tremor-specific monitoring.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent:**
  - **Propranolol** – 40 mg PO BD initially. Titrate weekly to max 320 mg/day divided BD or TDS.
  - *Note:* Consider sustained-release (Propranolol LA) 80 mg OD for compliance.
- **Common alternative:**
  - **Primidone** – 50 mg PO nocte for 1 week, then 50 mg BD; increase to 250 mg/day if needed (slow titration due to sedation).
  - *Can also use:* Gabapentin 300 mg TDS, Topiramate 25 mg BD (off-label).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Propranolol:** Avoid in asthma, COPD, symptomatic bradycardia, heart block, decompensated heart failure. May mask hypoglycaemic symptoms (diabetics).
  - **Primidone:** Significant sedation/dizziness; avoid abrupt withdrawal (seizure risk). Interacts with warfarin, contraceptives.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Propranolol:** Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.
  - **Primidone:** Caution in renal impairment – reduce dose.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Propranolol:** Prescribing to an undiagnosed asthmatic → acute bronchospasm. Always ask about asthma/reactive airway disease.
  - **Primidone:** Starting at 250 mg OD → severe sedation, ataxia, fall risk. Always start at 50 mg nocte.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Drug side effects: propranolol (bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm); primidone (sedation, dizziness, ataxia).
  - Falls due to tremor while carrying hot liquids (burns) or during medication-induced postural hypotension.
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive functional disability (inability to feed, write, dress).
  - Social embarrassment, anxiety, depression.
  - No reduced life expectancy (unlike Parkinson’s).
sections.Prognosis:
- Slowly progressive over decades; symptom severity varies widely.
- Does **not** affect mortality. Many patients live a normal lifespan with variable disability.
- Untreated, tremor gradually worsens; treatment rarely eliminates tremor, but reduces amplitude and improves function.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask: “Does alcohol make your tremor better?”** – classic for ET, but also ask about recent alcohol withdrawal (exaggerated physiological tremor).
- **Do a writing sample and spiral drawing** in the notes – objective measure for follow-up.
- **Check TFT and drug history** in every tremor patient before labelling essential tremor – many are iatrogenic or metabolic.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not diagnose ET if rest tremor or bradykinesia present** – that’s Parkinson’s until proven otherwise.
- **Do not start propranolol without checking for asthma** – ask specifically about symptoms (wheeze, nocturnal cough, exertional SOB).
- **Do not prescribe primidone in elderly without fall risk assessment** – sedation causes hip fractures. Use propranolol first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New rest tremor, stiffness, difficulty turning in bed.
  - Side effects from medications: severe bradycardia (pulse <50), syncope, wheeze (propranolol); excessive sedation or unsteady gait (primidone).
  - Sudden worsening of tremor or new neurological symptoms (headache, vision change, unilateral weakness).
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP for repeat BP/pulse monitoring after starting beta-blocker.
  - Refer to neurology if inadequate response to two drug trials, or diagnostic uncertainty.
  - Movement disorder specialist for botulinum toxin or DBS if severe.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/neurology on-call if:**
  - Tremor is part of a new neurological syndrome (e.g. acute unilateral tremor + weakness – possible stroke).
  - Suspected serotonin syndrome or NMS (tachycardia, fever, rigidity, raised CK, myoclonus).
  - Severe drug side effect: propranolol-induced heart block (bradycardia, hypotension) – NEWS2 ≥5.
- **ICU criteria:**
  - Need for rapid sequence intubation in NMS/serotonin syndrome.
  - Refractory hypoglycaemia or thyroid storm.
  - Any patient with tremor and GCS <13 or lactate >4 mmol/L – escalate immediately.

rawMarkdown:
## Essential Tremor
**One-liner:** A common, slowly progressive, bilateral action tremor (postural/kinetic) of hands ± head/voice; rare under 40, lifelong risk after 65.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy** – *single finding:* known diabetes + tremor + confusion/diaphoresis; bedside BM <3.0 mmol/L. Treat immediately with 50 ml 50% glucose IV (or 1 mg glucagon IM if no IV access).
- **Serotonin syndrome / Neuroleptic malignant syndrome** – *single finding:* tremor + fever + autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, myoclonus) + recent SSRI/antipsychotic use. Suspect; stop drug; get senior review – risk of rhabdomyolysis, seizure, death within hours.
- **Thyrotoxic crisis** – *single finding:* tremor + tachycardia (often >120/min) + thyroid goitre/exophthalmos + fever. Check TSH/FT4 urgently; treat with beta-blockade (propranolol) and carbimazole – arrhythmia/thyroid storm can be fatal.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral, symmetrical hand tremor – worse with action (holding a cup, writing, pointing) and sustained posture (arms outstretched), *absent at rest*.
  - Head tremor (yes-yes/no-no), voice tremor (quivering).
  - Tremor improves with alcohol (classic but not pathognomonic).
  - Gradual onset over years; family history in 50%.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No rest tremor, no bradykinesia, no rigidity, no postural instability (differentiates from Parkinson’s).
  - Tremor increases with anxiety, caffeine, fatigue.
  - Gait is normal (unless concomitant ageing).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Parkinson’s disease** – rest tremor (pill-rolling), unilateral onset, bradykinesia, rigidity, hypomimia.
  - **Drug-induced tremor** – recent/exposure to valproate, lithium, SSRIs, theophylline, beta-agonists, corticosteroids; tremor resolves on drug cessation.
  - **Physiological tremor (exaggerated)** – causes: anxiety, caffeine, hyperthyroidism, hypoglycaemia, alcohol withdrawal, fever.
  - **Cerebellar tremor** – intention tremor (gets worse towards target), past-pointing, dysdiadochokinesia, ataxic gait.
  - **Wilson’s disease** – early-onset tremor (<40), dystonia, Kayser-Fleischer rings, slurred speech; treatable if caught early.
- **Splitter:** **Rest tremor + bradykinesia** = Parkinson’s; **postural/kinetic tremor + head/voice involvement + normal gait** = Essential Tremor.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - BM (fingerstick glucose) – rule out hypoglycaemia.
  - Pulse, BP, temperature – look for thyrotoxicosis or serotonin syndrome.
  - Observe tremor at rest, posture, intention; ask patient to write a sentence.
  - Draw a spiral – assess severity (can repeat serially).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, glucose.
  - If young onset (<40): ceruloplasmin, serum copper, 24h urinary copper (rule out Wilson’s).
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **None** – diagnosis is clinical.
  - Second-line: **MRI brain** only if atypical features (unilateral, intention, early onset) to exclude structural lesion (e.g. cerebellar stroke, tumour, Wilson’s changes).
- **Special tests:**
  - **DAT SPECT** (DaTSCAN) – only if diagnostic uncertainty between ET and Parkinson’s (normal in ET, reduced in Parkinson’s).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Movement Disorder Society (MDS) criteria:**
    1. Bilateral action tremor of hands + forearms for ≥3 years.
    2. ± tremor of head, voice, legs.
    3. No rest tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, gait disturbance, or other neurological signs.
- **Never-miss test:** **BM glucose** – any tremor with confusion/sweating must have fingerstick glucose before anything else.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude and treat hypoglycaemia or toxidrome (see Killers First).
  - No specific therapy required for ET itself in acute setting.
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - If admitted for other reason: assess functional impact (eating, writing, dressing). Offer treatment only if disabling.
  - Start propranolol (first-line) if no contraindications (asthma, heart block, bradycardia). Titrate slowly.
  - Consider referral to neurology if atypical, refractory, or young.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **First-line:** Propranolol (see Drugs & Doses).
  - **Second-line:** Primidone (especially for hand tremor).
  - **Third-line:** Topiramate, gabapentin (off-label).
  - **Focal/refractory:** Botulinum toxin type A for head/voice tremor (must be done by movement disorder specialist).
  - **Severe/drug-resistant:** Deep brain stimulation (ventral intermediate nucleus of thalamus).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** No specific requirement.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Only if immobile – standard (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if weight >50 kg, avoid if high bleed risk).
  - **Diet/NBM:** No restriction unless undergoing procedure.
  - **Observations:** Standard NEWS chart – no tremor-specific monitoring.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent:**
  - **Propranolol** – 40 mg PO BD initially. Titrate weekly to max 320 mg/day divided BD or TDS.
  - *Note:* Consider sustained-release (Propranolol LA) 80 mg OD for compliance.
- **Common alternative:**
  - **Primidone** – 50 mg PO nocte for 1 week, then 50 mg BD; increase to 250 mg/day if needed (slow titration due to sedation).
  - *Can also use:* Gabapentin 300 mg TDS, Topiramate 25 mg BD (off-label).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Propranolol:** Avoid in asthma, COPD, symptomatic bradycardia, heart block, decompensated heart failure. May mask hypoglycaemic symptoms (diabetics).
  - **Primidone:** Significant sedation/dizziness; avoid abrupt withdrawal (seizure risk). Interacts with warfarin, contraceptives.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Propranolol:** Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.
  - **Primidone:** Caution in renal impairment – reduce dose.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Propranolol:** Prescribing to an undiagnosed asthmatic → acute bronchospasm. Always ask about asthma/reactive airway disease.
  - **Primidone:** Starting at 250 mg OD → severe sedation, ataxia, fall risk. Always start at 50 mg nocte.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Drug side effects: propranolol (bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm); primidone (sedation, dizziness, ataxia).
  - Falls due to tremor while carrying hot liquids (burns) or during medication-induced postural hypotension.
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive functional disability (inability to feed, write, dress).
  - Social embarrassment, anxiety, depression.
  - No reduced life expectancy (unlike Parkinson’s).

### Prognosis
- Slowly progressive over decades; symptom severity varies widely.
- Does **not** affect mortality. Many patients live a normal lifespan with variable disability.
- Untreated, tremor gradually worsens; treatment rarely eliminates tremor, but reduces amplitude and improves function.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask: “Does alcohol make your tremor better?”** – classic for ET, but also ask about recent alcohol withdrawal (exaggerated physiological tremor).
- **Do a writing sample and spiral drawing** in the notes – objective measure for follow-up.
- **Check TFT and drug history** in every tremor patient before labelling essential tremor – many are iatrogenic or metabolic.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not diagnose ET if rest tremor or bradykinesia present** – that’s Parkinson’s until proven otherwise.
- **Do not start propranolol without checking for asthma** – ask specifically about symptoms (wheeze, nocturnal cough, exertional SOB).
- **Do not prescribe primidone in elderly without fall risk assessment** – sedation causes hip fractures. Use propranolol first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New rest tremor, stiffness, difficulty turning in bed.
  - Side effects from medications: severe bradycardia (pulse <50), syncope, wheeze (propranolol); excessive sedation or unsteady gait (primidone).
  - Sudden worsening of tremor or new neurological symptoms (headache, vision change, unilateral weakness).
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP for repeat BP/pulse monitoring after starting beta-blocker.
  - Refer to neurology if inadequate response to two drug trials, or diagnostic uncertainty.
  - Movement disorder specialist for botulinum toxin or DBS if severe.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/neurology on-call if:**
  - Tremor is part of a new neurological syndrome (e.g. acute unilateral tremor + weakness – possible stroke).
  - Suspected serotonin syndrome or NMS (tachycardia, fever, rigidity, raised CK, myoclonus).
  - Severe drug side effect: propranolol-induced heart block (bradycardia, hypotension) – NEWS2 ≥5.
- **ICU criteria:**
  - Need for rapid sequence intubation in NMS/serotonin syndrome.
  - Refractory hypoglycaemia or thyroid storm.
  - Any patient with tremor and GCS <13 or lactate >4 mmol/L – escalate immediately.
